metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Migraine was estimated to affect approximately 1.2 billion individuals in 2021, with headache disorders remaining one of the top 15 causes of global disability-adjusted life-years from 2010 to 2021 [ 1 ]. The prevalence of mental health disorders, including depression, is estimated to be more than double in people with migraine compared with people without migraine [ 2 ]. Anxiety and depressive disorders consistently ranked in the top 25 causes of global disability-adjusted life-years from 2010 to 2021 [ 1 ], with the presence of psychiatric comorbidities having been shown to significantly increase the risk of migraine disease progression [ 3 , 4 ].

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Current evidence of preventive migraine treatments that target calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) in people with migraine and psychiatric comorbidities includes one placebo-controlled clinical trial in participants with migraine and comorbid major depressive disorder, as well as several post hoc analyses and real-world studies. The UNITE trial, a double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomized clinical trial in 353 participants, evaluated the efficacy and safety of fremanezumab in adults with migraine and comorbid major depressive disorder [ 5 ]; in the trial, fremanezumab resulted in significantly greater reductions in the number of monthly migraine days (MMDs) and depressive symptoms (via the 17-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale) during the first 12 weeks of treatment compared with placebo, and demonstrated greater improvements in patient-reported disease burden. Evidence with fremanezumab from the UNITE trial was preceded by and supported results from prospective real-world studies [ 6 – 9 ] and post hoc analyses using data from the phase 3 randomized HALO clinical trial [ 10 ]. Evidence supporting other anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies in people with migraine and psychiatric comorbidities includes a post hoc analysis of the real-world observational REVIEW study of 94 participants with chronic migraine (CM) treated with eptinezumab, which found large improvements as measured by the number of good days per month (as interpreted by the patient) [ 11 ], as well as a post hoc analysis of a real-world ambispective study of 102 participants with CM treated with erenumab, galcanezumab, onabotulinumtoxinA, or oral pharmacotherapy [ 12 ]. Interestingly, evidence from some studies suggests that the presence of comorbid psychiatric comorbidities such as anxiety and/or depression may decrease the likelihood or magnitude of migraine response to treatment [ 7 – 9 , 13 ]; however, this has not been consistently observed across analyses or anti-CGRP therapies [ 7 , 11 , 12 ]. For example, the post hoc analysis of the REVIEW study showed that the patient-reported presence of psychiatric comorbidities did not impact the real-world effectiveness of eptinezumab [ 11 ].

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Eptinezumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody that inhibits the action of CGRP and is approved for migraine prevention in adults [ 14 ]. The phase 3b DELIVER clinical trial demonstrated the efficacy and safety of eptinezumab for migraine prevention in participants with migraine for whom multiple traditional preventive treatments had failed [ 15 , 16 ]. Preventive benefits associated with eptinezumab were observed as early as Day 1 and through 72 weeks of treatment [ 15 , 16 ]. These post hoc analyses explored whether the short-term efficacy and long-term effectiveness of eptinezumab in subgroups of DELIVER participants who self-reported psychiatric comorbidities at screening, including depressive conditions, were reflective of the total DELIVER trial population.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: These post hoc analyses used data from DELIVER, a phase 3b clinical trial that evaluated the efficacy and safety of eptinezumab compared with placebo for the preventive treatment of migraine in adults with a history of preventive treatment failures [ 15 , 16 ]. DELIVER was a multicenter, parallel-group, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled clinical trial conducted in 96 sites across Europe and the US. The trial was conducted in compliance with current Good Clinical Practices as defined in the International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use guidelines, local regulatory requirements, and the Declaration of Helsinki policy statement. The local review board or a central institutional review board/ethics committee approved all study documentation at each of the 96 study sites across Europe and the US (Table S1 ). All participants provided written informed consent prior to trial participation. DELIVER is registered on ClinicalTrials. gov ( NCT04418765 ) and EudraCT (2019–004497–25). Full trial details, including the protocol and statistical analysis plan, have been published [ 15 , 16 ].

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: The trial comprised a 28–30-day screening period, a 24-week double-blind placebo-controlled period, and a 48-week dose-blinded extension period. Codes for randomization in the placebo-controlled and extension periods were assigned at the start of the trial, with randomization stratified by country and number of monthly headache days (MHDs) at baseline (≤ 14, > 14). Participants were randomized in a 1:1:1 ratio to receive eptinezumab 100 mg, 300 mg, or placebo, administered intravenously every 12 weeks during the placebo-controlled period (Weeks 1–24). For the dose-blinded extension period (Weeks 25–72), participants initially randomized to placebo were switched in a randomized manner (1:1 ratio) to receive eptinezumab 100 mg or 300 mg, administered intravenously every 12 weeks, while those initially randomized to eptinezumab continued their assigned dosing regimen.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Throughout each trial period, participants maintained a daily electronic diary (eDiary) in which they documented headache/migraine characteristics, symptoms, and associated acute medication intake.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Eligible participants were male or female adults (ages 18–75 years, inclusive) with onset of migraine at ≤ 50 years of age. Participants must have had a history of episodic migraine (EM) or CM for ≥ 12 months prior to the screening visit (per the International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition, criteria [ 17 ]) that was confirmed during the screening period based on eDiary data (CM: ≥ 15 headache days/month for > 3 months and ≥ 8 migraine days; EM: ≤ 14 headache days and ≥ 4 migraine days) [ 18 ]. Participants needed documented evidence (medical record or physician confirmation) of 2–4 preventive migraine medications in the past 10 years that were treatment failures due to inadequate efficacy, safety, and/or tolerability reasons, or contraindications. Medications that could be a prior failure included propranolol/metoprolol, topiramate, amitriptyline, flunarizine, candesartan, valproate/divalproex, and botulinum toxin A/B (if used for CM). At least one prior failure had to be due to inadequate efficacy with propranolol/metoprolol, topiramate, amitriptyline, flunarizine, or candesartan.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Prior failure on a treatment targeting the CGRP pathway was an exclusion criterion, as was prior failure on valproate/divalproex or botulinum toxin A if the treatment was not the most recent preventive medication prior to trial inclusion. Participants were excluded if they had a psychiatric condition that was uncontrolled and/or untreated for ≥ 6 months prior to the screening visit, a lifetime history of psychosis and/or mania in the last 5 years prior to the screening visit, or a current diagnosis or history of substance abuse or alcohol abuse (per the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, fifth edition, criteria [ 19 ]) for < 24 months prior to the screening visit. Additionally, participants at significant risk of suicide (defined using the Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale, as the participant answering yes to suicidal ideation question 4 or 5 or answering yes to suicidal behavior within the past 12 months) at the screening visit or baseline visit were excluded. The complete inclusion and exclusion criteria have been published previously [ 15 , 16 ].

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: The primary efficacy endpoint in DELIVER was the change from baseline in MMDs over Weeks 1–12. MMDs were derived from eDiary data, with a migraine day defined according to International Headache Society guidelines for controlled migraine prevention trials [ 20 ]. In addition to Weeks 1–12, MMDs were assessed at each 4-week interval (Weeks 1–4, 5–8, … 69–72) and at each 12-week interval (Weeks 13–24, 25–36, … 61–72) throughout the trial.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: A key secondary efficacy endpoint was the ≥ 50% migraine responder rate (MRR) over Weeks 1–12. The ≥ 50% MRRs represent the proportion of participants with a ≥ 50% reduction from baseline in MMDs. Similar to MMDs, ≥ 50% MRRs were assessed over each 4-week and 12-week interval throughout the trial.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: The Patient Global Impression of Change (PGIC) was a patient-reported outcome captured at Week 4, Week 12, and every 12 weeks thereafter to Week 72 [ 16 ]. PGIC comprises a single item asking participants to rate the change in their disease status since the start of the trial (i.e., in relation to activity limitations, symptoms, emotions, and overall quality of life), for which the 7-point response scale ranged from “very much improved” to “very much worse.” The proportion of participants reporting “much improved” or “very much improved” on the PGIC scale was assessed at each time point.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Analyses for the placebo-controlled period were conducted using the total efficacy population (full analysis set) and for the extension period were conducted using the extension population. The total efficacy population included all randomized participants who received study treatment, had a valid baseline assessment of MMDs, and had ≥ 1 valid 4-week assessment of MMDs during Weeks 1–12. The extension population included all randomized participants who received study treatment, had a valid baseline assessment of MMDs, and had ≥ 1 valid 4-week assessment of MMDs in the extension period. Within these populations, outcomes were analyzed in the subgroup who reported ≥ 1 co-occurring psychiatric condition at screening (“psychiatric comorbidity”) and the subgroup who reported ≥ 1 co-occurring psychiatric condition at screening that specified depression or depressive symptoms (“depressive conditions”).

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: The “psychiatric comorbidity” subgroups included participants who self-reported the presence of ≥ 1 psychiatric condition at the screening visit. Psychiatric conditions were those classified under the psychiatric disorders system organ class of the Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA, version 24.0). The “depressive conditions” subgroups included participants for whom ≥ 1 self-reported psychiatric condition at the screening visit was depression or depressive symptoms, including the following preferred terms: major depression, persistent depressive disorder, mixed anxiety and depressive disorder, depressed mood, depression, and depressive symptom. Co-occurring psychiatric conditions, including depressive conditions, were self-reported by participants at screening as part of the medical history and by a review of available medical records. No specific criteria for the duration of the psychiatric comorbidity were used for subgroup assignment.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Depending on the resulting sample sizes, it was of interest to evaluate outcomes in the psychiatric comorbidity and depressive conditions subgroups within subsets further defined by migraine type at baseline. Migraine types included EM (≤ 14 MHDs and ≥ 4 MMDs), CM (> 14 MHDs and ≥ 8 MMDs), and high-frequency EM or CM (HFEM + CM; ≥ 8 MMDs).

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Eptinezumab doses were pooled to improve the robustness of the statistical analyses of the efficacy outcomes, as a previous post hoc analysis did not find a statistical difference between 100 and 300 mg [ 21 ]. Thus, in these post hoc analyses, the “eptinezumab” treatment group includes all participants randomized to eptinezumab 100 mg or 300 mg during the placebo-controlled period. The “placebo” treatment group includes all participants randomized to placebo for the placebo-controlled period, then eptinezumab 100 mg or 300 mg for the extension period.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Changes from baseline in MMDs in this post hoc analysis were analyzed using a mixed model for repeated measures similar to the one used for the primary efficacy endpoint (2-sided; 95% confidence interval [CI]) [ 15 ]. The model included study month, country, stratification factor (baseline MHDs: ≤ 14, > 14), and treatment as factors, baseline score as a continuous covariate, and interaction terms for treatment-by-month, stratum-by-month, and baseline score-by-month. Estimates and tests across 12-week periods were computed using equal weights for each 4-week interval.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Analyses of dichotomous endpoints (i.e., ≥ 50% MRRs and proportion of participants improved per PGIC) were based on logistic regression models that included respective baseline score as a continuous covariate and stratification factor (baseline MHDs: ≤ 14, > 14) and treatment as factors. For each 4-week interval after baseline, ≥ 50% migraine responder status was determined based on the percentage change from baseline in MMDs. If MMD data were missing for a specific month, responder status for that month was considered missing. For 12-week intervals, responder status was calculated using available monthly data (even if some months within the interval had missing MMD values). Missing data for months with < 14 eDiary entry were imputed prior to analysis (as part of the original analysis), and for any month with ≥ 14 missing eDiary entries, MMD values were reported as missing. To analyze the proportion of participants reporting “much improved” or “very much improved,” missing data were not imputed.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Descriptive statistics are used to present the results; however, no p -values are reported for these post hoc analyses. Analyses were conducted using R Core Team (version 4.1.2; R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria) and RStudio Team (4.1; RStudio, PBC, Boston, MA, USA).

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Of 890 participants in the total efficacy population, 122 (13.7%) reported ≥ 1 co-occurring psychiatric condition of any kind at the screening visit. The most common psychiatric conditions in the total efficacy population were affective disorders ( n = 69/890, 7.8%), anxiety disorders ( n = 38/890, 4.3%), and sleep disorders ( n = 29/890, 3.3%) (Table 1 ). Of the 122 participants with ≥ 1 psychiatric comorbidity, 9 had 2 psychiatric comorbidities and 5 had 3 psychiatric comorbidities. Of the total efficacy population, 68 (7.6%) reported a depressive condition.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: The extension population included 858 participants who completed the Weeks 69–72 visit, of whom 115/858 (13.4%) had reported a psychiatric condition of any kind at screening, and 65/115 (56.5%) of those reporting a depressive condition completed the Weeks 69–72 visit.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Since the extension population comprises 96.4% of the total efficacy population, demographic and baseline characteristics are presented for the extension population, which was predominantly female (female, 89.6%; male, 10.4%) (Table 2 ). The characteristics of participants self-reporting a psychiatric comorbidity or depressive condition were generally similar to the overall extension population (Table 2 ); however, the psychiatric comorbidity and depressive condition subgroups had a slightly higher mean number of baseline MMDs (psychiatric: 15.3; depressive: 15.0; total: 13.7), higher mean number of years with migraine (psychiatric: 19.8; depressive: 20.4; total: 17.7), fewer participants for whom > 2 prior preventive treatments failed (psychiatric: 29.6%; depressive: 26.2% total: 38.2%), and more participants with CM (psychiatric: 59.1%; depressive: 56.9%; total: 45.3%) (Table 2 ).

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: While the per-arm sample sizes precluded meaningful analysis, the baseline characteristics analyzed by migraine type (EM, CM, or HFEM + CM; Table S2) were similar to the respective overarching subgroup (i.e., psychiatric condition or depressive condition subgroup), suggesting results would be similar across migraine type.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: During the placebo-controlled period, mean reductions from baseline in MMDs were greater with eptinezumab than with placebo across populations (Fig. 1 ). The differences from placebo numerically increased from Weeks 1–12 to 13–24 across populations and were numerically greater in participants self-reporting a psychiatric comorbidity or depressive condition than in the total efficacy population (Fig. 1 ).

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: During the extension period, long-term eptinezumab treatment in DELIVER resulted in consistent and ongoing reductions in MMDs across populations (Fig. 2 ; Figure S1 ). Participants with a psychiatric comorbidity reported a similar reduction in MMDs from a higher number of MMDs from baseline through the extension period compared with the extension population, as did participants with depressive conditions (Fig. 2 ). A similar overall reduction in MMDs was observed at Weeks 25–28 in the placebo-to-eptinezumab treatment group as in the eptinezumab treatment group among the three populations, with mean MMDs during the extension period relatively similar between treatment groups at each time point (Figure S1 ).

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: During the placebo-controlled period, ≥ 50% MRRs were higher with eptinezumab than with placebo, with a ≥ fourfold odds ratio (OR) of response with eptinezumab across time points and populations (Fig. 3 ). The OR of response over Weeks 13–24 with eptinezumab versus placebo was > 9 for participants reporting any psychiatric comorbidity (OR = 9.8) and for participants reporting depressive conditions (OR = 9.2) and was 4.1 for the total efficacy population.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: During the extension period, ≥ 50% MRRs were generally consistent between treatment groups in the extension population (Fig. 4 a) but were slightly higher in the eptinezumab treatment group than in the placebo-to-eptinezumab treatment group in the extension subgroup with any psychiatric comorbidity (Fig. 4 b) and extension subgroup with depressive conditions (Fig. 4 c).

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: During the placebo-controlled period, the proportion of participants improving per PGIC (i.e., reported “much improved” or “very much improved”) was generally consistent across populations, with eptinezumab having > fivefold OR of response across comparisons versus placebo (Fig. 5 ). The OR of improvement per PGIC over Weeks 1–12 or 13–24 with eptinezumab versus placebo was largest in participants with depressive conditions, followed by participants with any psychiatric comorbidity, and then the total efficacy population (Fig. 5 ).

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: During the extension period, participants who switched from placebo to eptinezumab reported similar rates of improvement per PGIC following the first eptinezumab infusion (Week 36) to participants treated with eptinezumab for the entire trial (Fig. 6 a). In both treatment groups, the proportion of participants improving per PGIC slightly increased through Week 72 (eptinezumab: 70% at Week 36 to 79% at Week 72; placebo-to-eptinezumab: 67% at Week 36 to 78% at Week 72). Similar patterns were noted in the extension subgroups with any psychiatric comorbidity (Fig. 6 b) and with depressive conditions (Fig. 6 c), although a numerically lower response was observed in participants switched from placebo to eptinezumab in those with any psychiatric comorbidity. Similar to the overall extension population, the placebo-to-eptinezumab groups in both subgroups showed results in the extension period that were similar to those in the eptinezumab group in the placebo-controlled period.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: This post hoc analysis of the DELIVER trial examined the efficacy and long-term (72-week) effectiveness of eptinezumab on MMDs, ≥ 50% MRRs, and the proportion of participants improving per PGIC across the placebo-controlled and extension periods in participants reporting a psychiatric condition at screening and in participants reporting depressive conditions at screening, and explored whether the efficacy profiles were reflective of the total efficacy population. Across outcomes during the placebo-controlled period, eptinezumab consistently demonstrated numerically greater efficacy than placebo across all populations. Relative to the total efficacy population, the impact of eptinezumab versus placebo on MMDs, ≥ 50% MRRs, and the proportion of participants improving per PGIC—which has been shown to be correlated with treatment continuation in real-world practice [ 22 ]—was numerically greater in participants reporting any psychiatric comorbidities at baseline, including those with depressive conditions. Similar data have been shown in real-world evidence populations, where anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies have improved depressive symptoms regardless of the improvement in migraine and headache days, although eptinezumab was not included in that analysis [ 23 ]. Conversely, when looking at large real-world cohorts, absence of depression has been proven to be correlated with better treatment response after 6 months [ 24 ].

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: In the extension population and the corresponding subgroups reporting a psychiatric condition or reporting depressive conditions, participants assigned to eptinezumab for all dosing intervals consistently demonstrated numerically greater MMD reductions and higher proportions of ≥ 50% migraine responders and participants improving per PGIC over the first 24 weeks than participants assigned to placebo with a switch to eptinezumab, in alignment with results from the total efficacy population. Additionally, a treatment switch from placebo to eptinezumab was associated with a similar magnitude of change in MMDs and similar ≥ 50% MRRs and PGIC improvement as observed with initial eptinezumab treatment. Participants switching from placebo to eptinezumab showed similar ≥ 50% MRRs and proportion of participants improving per PGIC to the eptinezumab group; however, those with psychiatric comorbidities had somewhat milder responses, which may suggest a requirement for longer treatment periods.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Demographics and baseline characteristics suggested that the subgroups with psychiatric conditions had slightly higher disease severity than participants in the extension population, as characterized by approximately two additional MMDs and 2 years more with migraine and having more participants with CM (yet fewer participants with > 2 prior failures). There is a reported link between higher psychiatric comorbidity and greater burden of disease [ 2 , 25 ]. Results from the Atlas of Migraine in Spain, a 2018 online survey of 468 participants with migraine, demonstrated a significant positive linear correlation between the number of headache days per month and the risk of anxiety, depression, and severe disability [ 26 ]. Such comorbidities also correlate with poorer health outcomes, including reduced quality of life, increased risk of medication overuse, greater financial burden, and worse long-term response [ 2 , 25 , 27 ].

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: Participants reporting a psychiatric condition had more MMDs at baseline (more likely to fulfill CM criteria than EM criteria), including a similar magnitude of MMD reduction, and retained a numerically higher number of MMDs. Although analyzing subgroups defined by migraine type (EM, CM, or HFEM + CM) was of clinical interest, small sample sizes resulting from the criteria precluded meaningful analysis. However, similarities in baseline characteristics between the psychiatric comorbidity and depressive condition subgroups and their respective migraine type subsets (Table S2) suggest results would be similar across migraine types.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: The percentage of participants with one or more psychiatric comorbidities in this trial was 13.7%, substantially below the prevalence of people with migraine with at least one psychiatric comorbidity in the general population, with reports ranging from 23 to 75% [ 27 – 29 ]. In a pooled analysis of two phase 3 randomized controlled trials, EVOLVE-1 and EVOLVE-2, in participants with EM (4–14 MHDs), anxiety and/or depression was reported in 26.0% (461/1773) of participants [ 30 ]. In the REGAIN trial of participants with CM (≥ 15 MHDs, ≥ 8 of which had migraine-like features), the prevalence of anxiety and/or depression was 28.4% (316/1113). Compared with the general population and other migraine clinical trials, the prevalence of psychiatric comorbidities in migraine participants was underrepresented in this study [ 30 ], possibly related to under-reporting by participants.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: These data are subject to limitations inherent to post hoc analyses, including that all results are descriptive and exploratory; thus, these results are not powered for definitive conclusions and warrant further investigation. However, statistical methodology for analyzing trial endpoints was prespecified in the trial protocol. Generalizability of the study population is limited by the inclusion/exclusion criteria and demographic breakdown of trial participants (primarily female, white, and European), as well as the type of psychiatric condition reported by participants at screening. Thus, the present findings may not reflect a broader population of people who live with migraine and have a psychiatric comorbidity. The sample sizes for the psychiatric comorbidity and depressive conditions subgroups were relatively small, and psychiatric conditions were self-reported by participants and could only be cross-validated with medical records if those records were available. There is a chance that conditions, for example, may also be related to symptoms of their migraine disease. Moreover, the self-reporting of psychiatric comorbidities may influence participants’ responses on patient-reported outcome measures, including how participants perceive their level of disease burden and impression of change with treatment. The sample size could have been augmented by reviewing concomitant medications that overlap with psychiatric conditions such as mood disorders, but the reason for concomitant medication use was not captured. Although analyzing subgroups defined by migraine type (EM, CM, or HFEM + CM) or defined by other psychiatric conditions (e.g., anxiety, which has been shown to be more robustly associated with increased migraine risk than depression [ 31 ]) is of clinical interest, small sample sizes resulting from the criteria preclude meaningful analysis. The finding that ~ 10% fewer participants with > 2 prior treatment failures were reported in the psychiatric comorbidity and depressive condition subgroups than in the total extension population is not fully understood; possibly, participants had tried fewer treatments historically. Moreover, while the EQ-5D-5L anxiety/depression subscale was captured, few participants had elevated anxiety/depression scores, even among those with psychiatric comorbidities or depressive conditions.

metadata.title: Sustained Efficacy of Eptinezumab in Participants with Migraine for Whom Prior Preventive Treatments Failed and Who Self-reported Psychiatric Comorbidities: Post Hoc Analysis of the Placebo-controlled DELIVER Trial.

text: This post hoc analysis of the phase 3b DELIVER trial found consistent short- and long-term (72-week) effectiveness of eptinezumab across changes from baseline in MMDs, ≥ 50% MRRs, and rate of improvement per PGIC in participants who self-reported a psychiatric condition at screening and participants who self-reported depressive conditions at screening. Results for the psychiatric comorbidity and depressive condition subgroups were reflective of the total efficacy population, including the greater effectiveness of eptinezumab compared with placebo during the 24-week placebo-controlled period. Overall, the presence of psychiatric comorbidities, including depressive conditions, did not appear to impact the efficacy of eptinezumab in participants with migraine.

text: Esophageal cancer is a highly aggressive malignancy with a formidable global health burden, and the morbidity and mortality of esophageal cancer patients in China are the highest in the world. 1 Most patients with esophageal cancer seek medical treatment because of progressive dysphagia. When they are diagnosed, they are in the middle and late stages of the disease, and the incidence of malnutrition is as high as 60%-85%. 2 For stage II and III esophageal cancer, preoperative neoadjuvant treatment, primarily consisting of concurrent chemotherapy, immunotherapy and radiotherapy, has been established as the standard of care prior to curative-intent surgery. 3 This comprehensive treatment strategy significantly improves pathological response rates and overall survival. However, preoperative chemotherapy/radiotherapy is also a double-edged sword that is related to nausea, vomiting, radiation esophagitis and other symptoms (such as reduced taste acuity and xerostomia), which further affect the intake of nutrients and have a negative effect on patients’ nutritional status, therapeutic effect, and prognosis. 4

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Maintaining adequate nutritional status during preoperative neoadjuvant treatment is paramount. Robust nutritional support is directly correlated with enhanced treatment tolerance, reduced incidences of severe adverse events, lower postoperative complication rates (for example, anastomotic fistula, infection), and improved overall quality of life. 5 , 6 Therefore, active nutritional therapy is highly important for the treatment and prognosis of patients with esophageal cancer and has become an indispensable part of comprehensive cancer treatment. 7 , 8 To improve the nutritional status of these patients, dietary guidance is considered one of the most economical and effective methods. 9 Its main purpose is to improve dietary compliance by helping patients understand the importance of a designated diet and nutritional needs. Consequently, trusted nutrition advice and information, typically delivered by nutritionists or nutrition nurses upon hospital admission, aims to equip patients with the knowledge and strategies to navigate the complex dietary challenges posed by their treatment, such as dysphagia, nausea, taste alterations, and early satiety.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Although medical staff generally realize the importance of preoperative nutritional status and implement dietary guidance and recommendations, a major clinical problem still exists; that is, patients’ actual compliance with dietary guidance is often ignored. In clinical practice, patients’ compliance with the suggested diet plan is often poor and varies greatly. 10 , 11 Studies have shown that more than 20% of cancer patients’ dietary compliance becomes worse after chemotherapy/radiotherapy, which leads to the deterioration of tumor-related malnutrition, which not only reduces the treatment effect and quality of life of patients but also increases their medical expenses and mortality. 12 , 13 Dietary compliance is an essential prerequisite for effective nutritional interventions. Dietary adherence may be influenced by a complex, multifaceted interplay of factors that extend beyond simple comprehension. 14 , 15 At present, the understanding of these factors, especially deep-rooted perceptions and cognitive determinants from the patient’s perspective, is still limited and needs in-depth exploration.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Health belief is very important for patients to cultivate and maintain health behavior. 16 The health belief model (HBM) is one of the most commonly used models for understanding people’s medical compliance behavior and mainly includes six elements: perceived susceptibility, perceived severity, perceived benefits, perceived barriers, cues to action and self-efficacy. 17 This model focuses on the psychological changes of patients and suggests that if patients have beliefs related to health and disease and take the initiative to change risky behaviors, engaging in behaviors that are beneficial to disease health is guaranteed to achieve disease improvement. Some reviews and meta-analyses have confirmed the effectiveness of the HBM in predicting health behavior. 18 , 19 As cancer has the highest incidence of malnutrition, at present, many quantitative studies have been carried out on the nutritional management of esophageal cancer patients. 20 However, there are few qualitative studies on the dietary compliance of patients with esophageal cancer worldwide. Compared with quantitative research, qualitative research can better understand the real thoughts and feelings of patients and thus better analyze the specific influencing factors that affect compliance with dietary guidance for patients with esophageal cancer during preoperative neoadjuvant chemo-/radiotherapy.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Addressing dietary adherence is inherently a multidisciplinary challenge that extends beyond the scope of clinical nutrition alone. It requires the integrated efforts of surgeons, nurses, dietitians, psychologists, and social workers to effectively manage the complex interplay of physical symptoms, psychological distress, social support, and cultural beliefs that shape a patient’s eating behavior during treatment. Therefore, this study intends to analyze the influencing factors of dietary guidance compliance in esophageal cancer patients during neoadjuvant therapy from six aspects through descriptive research, taking the HBM as the theoretical framework to further guide and intervene in dietary management, effectively improve dietary compliance, and improve the feasibility, safety and final curative effect of follow-up surgery.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: This descriptive qualitative study used face-to-face semistructured in-depth interviews. The reporting of this study adhered to the Consolidated Criteria for Reporting Qualitative Studies (COREQ) checklist. 21

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: This study enrolled participants from the Thoracic Surgery Department of a large tertiary cancer hospital in central China between August and October 2025. This hospital, the leading cancer center in Henan Province, has a capacity of 3000 inpatient beds. During their initial consultation, potentially eligible patients were briefed on the study’s aims and procedure. Those who expressed interest were subsequently invited to enroll. The inclusion criteria were patients (1) ≥18 years of age; (2) had a confirmed diagnosis of esophageal cancer; (3) received preoperative neoadjuvant chemotherapy and/or radiotherapy for more than one cycle; (4) had basic reading and comprehension skills; and (6) were able to provide written informed consent. Potential subjects were excluded if they (1) had a diagnosis of any other cancer or (2) were unaware of their own disease. A purposive sampling strategy was employed to recruit participants and gather data, ensuring the inclusion of diverse perspectives across variables such as sex, age, cancer stage, educational background, and marital status. The sample size was determined by the saturation principle, which refers to the absence of new information from interviews. 22

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: A research team comprising two masters of nursing students, two clinical nurse specialists in nutrition, one professional nutritionist, and two nursing administrators, was assembled for this study. This multidisciplinary composition ensured a broad spectrum of expertise in both clinical care and nutriology. The initial interview guide was developed on the basis of the HBM and was informed by a comprehensive literature review and consultations with experts. The initial interview guideline was partially revised to form a formal interview guideline after pilot interviews with two patients ( Figure 1 ). All one-on-one, semistructured interviews were conducted by the first author, a female scholar with extensive experience in qualitative research. The interviews took place in a private conference room within the hospital to ensure confidentiality and minimize interruptions. Prior to the formal interview, the researcher dedicated time to building rapport with each participant. Notably, the interviewer was not involved in the direct clinical care of the participants, which helped foster a relaxed atmosphere. Throughout the interviewing procedure, the interviewer maintained a neutral attitude, carefully avoiding leading questions or suggestive statements.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: An audio recording device was used to record the entirety of each interview. Throughout the interview process, the sequence and content were flexibly adjusted on the basis of the participants’ responses, keeping relevance to the topic. The researcher also recorded nonverbal expressions, such as facial expressions and body language. All communication was conducted in Mandarin, with participants confirming that there were no language barriers. The interviews lasted between 30 and 50 minutes on average. At the end of each interview, the researcher provided a brief summary of the discussion and invited the participants to offer clarifications or additional comments to enhance the robustness of the data. After each interview, an interview memo was promptly completed, aiming to clearly document methodological decisions and potential biases during the study. No repeated interviews were conducted.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Ethical approval for this study was granted by the hospital’s ethics committee in accordance with the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki (2022–345). Prior to the interviews, all the participants received a detailed explanation of the study’s purpose and significance and provided written informed consent. They were also informed that they could withdraw from the study at any time without any consequence to their care. To ensure confidentiality, all audio recordings were securely deleted after transcription.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: The analysis was conducted manually without the use of any software package. This approach was chosen to allow us to extend the theoretical propositions of the HBM while remaining open to emergent findings. A directed content analysis approach was used to code the textual data, following a structured five-phase procedure. 23 (1) Immersion: Two researchers independently read and re-read the transcripts to gain a holistic sense of the data. (2) Initial coding using the HBM: A preliminary coding framework was developed based on the six core constructs of the HBM. The researchers then independently coded all transcripts, deductively assigning segments of text that corresponded to these predefined categories. (3) Identifying emergent categories (inductive coding): Crucially, to prevent the HBM from overly constraining our analysis, we actively sought and coded data that were relevant to the research question but did not fit neatly into the existing HBM constructs. These data segments were set aside and later analyzed inductively to identify new, emergent subcategories or categories. (4) Categories refinement and framework adjustment: Through iterative discussions within the research team, the initial deductive codes and the emergent inductive codes were compared, contrasted, and synthesized. This constant comparative process allowed us to refine the subcategories, adjust the coding framework dynamically, and ensure that the final thematic structure was both grounded in the HBM and truly representative of the participants’ voices. Any discrepancies in coding or interpretation were resolved through team discussions until a consensus was reached. (5) Synthesizing and interpreting: we established clear relationships between the coded text and the final categories/subcategories, selecting the most illustrative participant quotations to support our interpretations.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: The rigor of the qualitative study was assured via credibility, dependability, confirmability, and transferability. 24 Credibility was achieved by utilizing an interview guide developed from a thorough literature review and administered by an experienced qualitative interviewer. Furthermore, credibility was strengthened by incorporating direct participant quotations in the results, and findings were shared with participants for feedback. To enhance dependability and confirmability, all interviews were conducted by a researcher, and the data were independently analyzed and coded by two researchers. Any disagreements in the analysis were resolved through group discussion. Transferability was supported by providing a detailed description of the research methodology, including data collection and analysis processes, enabling others to judge the relevance of findings to different contexts. Moreover, a self-reflective attitude was upheld by the research team to minimize subjective bias and to ensure that the analysis remained grounded in and truly representative of the participants’ own perspectives and feelings.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Finally, saturation was reached for 15 patients because no new information appeared. Patients were coded as P1–P15, and more detailed characteristics of the participants are shown in Table 1 . Six categories and 15 corresponding subcategories were identified, which collectively illustrate the multifaceted factors influencing adherence to dietary guidance. The structure is organized around the core constructs of the HBM ( Table 2 ).

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Patients realized that they were at nutritional risk through direct and visible signals from their bodies. When patients interpret “malnutrition” through physical changes in their daily lives, the abstract medical concept is transformed into a genuine physical experience. Look at my belt; it has shrunk several buttonholes. These trousers keep slipping down when I wear them. It is as if they were not mine. I know that I have lost a lot of weight recently. (P1) When I wash my face, I touch it, and there is no meat on my face. When I look in the mirror, I can hardly recognize myself. I suspect it is because I did not eat well. (P12)

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Patients construct their nutritional risk perceptions through objective information and professional warnings provided by external medical systems. Compared with the immediacy of body signals, this cognition may be more forward-looking and rational. However, its effect depends on the clarity of information, the intensity of communication and the trust of patients in medical authority. The nurses asked me to weigh myself every week, and they always emphasized the importance of diet and nutrition. (P11) The nutritionist came to talk to me specifically, saying that at this stage, whether my nutrition is adequate or not makes an enormous difference in the subsequent treatment effect and recovery. (P10)

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Owing to the lack of relevant knowledge, some patients are often unaware of the danger to the treatment and rehabilitation of diseases when they are malnourished, which leads to poor dietary compliance. Patients tend to underestimate their nutritional risk and believe that they will not have nutritional problems or that the problems are not so serious. I think what you said is too serious. I just have no appetite these days, and I do not want to eat. I will eat more when I get better in a few days. How can I be so easily malnourished? (P4) I used to be in good health, and I do not think there was any problem with my nutrition. In addition, the most important thing is still medicine and surgery. Nutrition and exercise are just supplementary. (P14)

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Some patients with esophageal cancer agree that noncompliance with dietary guidance may have adverse consequences for their health and disease treatment. The most direct and strongest concern of patients is that nutritional problems interrupt or delay their core anticancer treatment. Second, patients feel the weakness and fatigue caused by malnutrition from personal experience. There was a man in the same ward who stopped chemotherapy for a week because of his weakness and low white blood cells. I cannot be like him. (P2) I was only halfway through my radiotherapy when the doctor told me that my physical condition was deteriorating too rapidly and asked if I could hold on. If I gave up halfway, all the money would be wasted, and all the suffering would be in vain. I must eat well from now on and have more nutritious food. (P6) I feel so weak that I have to lean against the wall and rest for a while when I walk from the bedside to the toilet. I know that if I do not eat something nutritious, my condition will get worse and worse. (P8)

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Esophageal cancer patients may experience a series of symptoms of discomfort due to multiple factors, such as tumor consumption, organ and tissue atrophy, and treatment. Some patients say that the dietary guidance provided by medical staff helps them control their weight and various physiological indices, improve their physical function and improve their quality of life. I have found that as long as I force myself to eat more every day, my spirit will be much better that day. If I throw up, take a break and then eat again, it is fine. Otherwise, with an empty stomach, I can only lie in bed, feeling dizzy and disoriented. (P3) When I can eat something, I feel much better and do not consider myself a patient anymore. During this period, I gained 1 kilogram, and the test results this time were better than last time. I’m very happy. (P5)

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Some patients regard food as the essential energy and material basis to support them throughout the entire cancer treatment process. They consider maintaining their nutritional status a mandatory prerequisite and an imperative “task” for obtaining the opportunity for a final curative surgery. I will force myself to eat as the doctor suggests. Good nutrition is the ticket for surgery. I weigh myself every day. (P11) Only after I have eaten well will the doctor dare to operate on me, and the success rate of the operation will also be higher. No matter how uncomfortable I feel, I must keep eating in order to recover smoothly. (P6)

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: The vast majority of patients mentioned that after chemotherapy/radiotherapy, they experienced symptoms such as decreased appetite, nausea, vomiting, heartburn, and altered taste, which affected their ability to eat. These symptoms made it difficult for them to follow the dietary advice given by medical staff. For several days after each chemotherapy session, I had no appetite and did not feel like eating. After a while when my body got a little better and I could eat a bit, it was time to come back to the hospital for another round of chemotherapy. (P7) I have remembered all your dietary suggestions, but since I started the treatment, my appetite has decreased even more. I feel full after eating just a little. In addition, I always have a strange taste in my mouth. (P9)

text: A few patients indicated that their illness exacerbated the financial burden on their already poor families, making it difficult for them to follow the dietary guidance provided by medical staff. That nutritional supplement costs a lot per box. The doctor said to take it every day. It is like drinking money. The surgery fee is also very expensive. Our family truly cannot afford it. (P13) After I got sick, I stopped going out to work, and there was no other income at home. It would be very difficult for me to follow the nurse’s advice and have a balanced diet with meat, eggs, milk, vegetables, and fruits every day. (P15)

text: Some patients may experience significant psychological stress, such as anxiety, depression and despondency. Moreover, they expressed that their illness has become a burden to their families, so they tend to be accommodating in their food choices, and it is difficult for them to adhere to the dietary guidance provided by medical staff. Just being alive is enough. I will eat whatever I want and skip what I do not. I do not want to worry so much about food. (P4) Every time I come to the hospital, my family members come to accompany me. The whole family revolves around me. I know I have caused them a lot of trouble, so I usually eat whatever they prepare. Seeing my family so tired, I feel so sorry for them. (P5)

text: Eating habits are deeply rooted in a person’s culture, family, emotions and daily life, so when the advice of medical staff conflicts with these deep-rooted habits, patients experience complex struggles. In addition to eating habits affecting patients’ eating behavior, traditional Chinese dietary concepts also have an impact on patients. I love to drink porridge, but now the nurse says not to drink it, which is easy to raise blood sugar. She asked me to drink milk and vegetables instead. I’m not used to drinking these. (P10) I do not eat fruits, although they are rich in vitamins. In Chinese medicine, fruits are cold food and are not good for health. (P8)

text: In the theory of the HBM, perceived susceptibility and perceived severity are usually attributed to perceived threats. 25 This study revealed that some patients tend to underestimate the severity of the problem because of a lack of relevant knowledge and regard malnutrition as a temporary and reversible “appetite problem” or believe that its importance is far lower than that of drugs and surgery, which is an obstacle to patients’ dietary compliance. Previous studies have shown that the nutritional knowledge of cancer patients is low and that the nutritional knowledge literacy level of patients is related to sociodemographic factors such as education level, sex, age, and economic status. 26 Patients with higher education levels or younger patients can learn disease-related knowledge more actively, obtain medical information in more diverse ways, understand the development and prognosis of the disease more comprehensively, and have higher compliance with treatment. Healthcare workers should provide personalized nutrition education and dietary guidance to patients through diverse channels, such as online and offline, on the basis of their different sociodemographic backgrounds. In contrast, some patients can perceive nutritional risks through their own body signals and external medical information. Research has shown that the greater an individual’s perception of health threats is, the greater the possibility that they will adopt positive health behaviors. 27 Healthcare providers should increase patients’ susceptibility to nutritional risks through specific cases and promote their timely and effective implementation of dietary management. Many patients in this study perceived that the harm caused by malnutrition was related to two consequences: one was the direct interruption of the anticancer treatment process, and the other was the decline in their own physical functions. Given that intuitive body signals are the most primitive driving force for behavior change, personalized nutrition support strategies should not only provide online dietary guidance and other services with the help of smartphone applications based on digital health but also utilize decision-making aids to directly link the treatment goals that patients are most concerned about with their most immediate physical sensations.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: This study clearly indicates that the perceived barriers faced by esophageal cancer patients following dietary guidance are complex and multifaceted, revealing why some patients recognize the importance of nutrition but find it difficult to adhere to dietary guidance. Research has shown that cancer patients often suffer from various problems, such as loss of appetite, nausea and vomiting, and changes in taste during chemotherapy. 28 In the interviews, the vast majority of patients also believed that the above symptoms were the greatest obstacles affecting their interest in eating and compliance with diet. Healthcare professionals should use professional appetite assessment tools to quantitatively evaluate patients’ appetite, promptly identify problems and implement relevant interventions. 29 , 30 For patients with a reduced appetite, commonly used appetite-stimulating drugs are often prescribed. Moreover, alternative Chinese therapies such as moxibustion, acupoint application, decoctions, or nonpharmacological interventions such as psychological therapy can be combined for treatment. 31 In addition, frequently changing the types of food, the combination of dishes and cooking methods, to enhance the stimulation of vision, smell and taste, increase patients’ interest in eating and improve their compliance with diet, is also recommended. The economic burden is also a realistic issue. In the interviews, some patients lost confidence in following dietary guidance because of the relatively high cost of treatment. It is necessary to expand the reimbursement scope of basic medical insurance and increase the reimbursement rate to reduce the medical expenses of patients and their families. Clinical intervention must incorporate economic assessment into routine nutrition screening and actively link social resources, such as charitable aid projects. Otherwise, any refined nutrition plan would be nothing but a castle in the air for patients mired in economic hardship.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Moreover, patients’ dietary concepts largely influence their dietary behaviors. When scientific nutritional advice conflicts with patients’ deeply rooted cultural dietary habits and traditional beliefs, patients may find it hard to accept. 32 In traditional Chinese medicine, overeating heaty food (“Shang huo” in Chinese) and “Fa wu” (meaning nourishing and stimulating food) are bad for patients. These findings suggest that medical staff need to pay attention to patients’ preferences and cultural context when providing nutritional care guidance. Finally, this study revealed that some patients have relatively serious psychological problems. Patients’ anxiety, fear or guilt caused by worrying that their illness would become a burden to the family leads to low self-efficacy; thus, they give up compliance with dietary guidance. Good self-efficacy is the intrinsic motivation for patients to adhere to dietary guidance. 33 Patients with high self-efficacy become active leaders in their own health management, proactively exploring methods that suit them. However, patients with low self-efficacy develop fear and resistance toward eating after repeated failures. When providing dietary guidance, medical staff should pay attention to the psychological burden and self-efficacy of patients. They can encourage patients to express themselves and regulate their emotions by organizing expressive writing interventions or supportive-expressive discussion groups. 34 Individuals with low self-efficacy can also rebuild their confidence and ability to overcome difficulties by setting small, achievable goals, through methods such as mindfulness-based stress reduction, and by constantly summarizing successful experiences that suit them. 35 , 36

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: This study revealed that in terms of perceived benefits, patients’ understanding of the benefits that following dietary guidance can bring constitutes the driving force for them to overcome their eating disorders. Parwati et al noted that only when individuals fully recognize the positive outcomes associated with healthy behaviors are they more likely to adhere to health management behaviors. 37 When patients associate their diet with improved mental state and reduced fatigue, this intuitive physical perception can motivate their compliance with dietary regimens. Second, some patients link their nutritional status with the success rate of surgery and the long-term survival rate. The benefits of nutrition for disease recovery can help them overcome eating disorders better. In clinical practice, healthcare providers should not only consciously help patients identify and record the short-term positive changes caused by nutritional behaviors but also emphasize the long-term benefits of nutrition, thereby establishing a continuous and multilevel positive feedback loop. Notably, in China, clinical nutrition work is still in a stage of gradual development. 38 This may be related to the lack of unified and standardized dietary guidance, which leads to contradictory and unclear dietary advice being provided to patients. Medical staff and clinical nutritionists are the main sources for patients to obtain nutrition education and dietary guidance. Medical staff should first increase their own reserves of nutrition knowledge, intensify educational efforts, and diversify educational forms to help patients develop good and healthy dietary habits. 39 The development of visual dietary guidance tools to increase the intuitiveness and operability of the guidance is recommended.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: It is well known that social support strongly influences patients’ dietary concepts and habits. 40 Preoperative neoadjuvant therapy for patients with esophageal cancer is a long-term process that requires the full mobilization of multidimensional social support systems to encourage patients to improve their dietary compliance. In terms of family support, patients have the most contact with their family members. Family members need to not only guide and encourage patients mentally but also supervise and urge them in terms of diet, playing a positive motivating role. Research shows that family-oriented psychosocial interventions are more effective for patients’ physical and mental health than patient-centered interventions or routine care. 41 Therefore, medical staff should also attach importance to providing nutritional education to the families of patients, increasing their knowledge reserves. Moreover, encouraging the exchange of successful experiences among patients is particularly important. Peer support, as a unique form of social support, plays an irreplaceable role in which neither medical staff nor family members can match. 42 Because of their shared or similar experiences with illness, fellow patients offer unparalleled empathy and credibility. The communication among patients can enhance their self-efficacy. However, medical staff should pay attention to the accuracy of such information and promptly correct any cognitive misunderstandings that patients may have.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: The findings of this study underscore the necessity of a multidisciplinary approach to improving dietary adherence. For oncologists and surgeons, the results highlight the need to move beyond simply prescribing nutrition, and instead, explicitly communicate the link between nutritional status and tangible treatment outcomes (eg., avoiding treatment delays, ensuring surgical candidacy) to enhance patients’ perceived threat and benefits. Nurses and dietitians, who are at the frontline of patient education, should adopt a dual strategy: providing clear, professional guidance while also assessing and addressing patients’ cultural beliefs and psychological barriers. They can facilitate “cues to action” by involving family caregivers in education sessions and by connecting patients with suitable peer support. Furthermore, the significant role of psychological distress and self-efficacy calls for the early integration of psychologists or psychiatric liaison nurses into the nutrition care team. Interventions such as mindfulness or setting small, achievable goals can be powerful tools for rebuilding a patient’s sense of mastery over their eating. Finally, social workers have a crucial role in identifying patients facing financial toxicity and linking them with social resources or charitable aid, thereby mitigating a major “perceived barrier.” By working synergistically, this multidisciplinary team can address the full spectrum of factors—from the physiological to the psychological and social—that determine a patient’s ability to adhere to dietary guidance.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Despite these insightful findings, this study has several limitations that should be considered when the results are interpreted. First, the qualitative descriptive design and relatively small sample size of 15 participants, all of whom were recruited from a single clinical center, may limit the generalizability of the findings. Future research could use a multicenter design to increase the diversity and representativeness of the participants. Second, the data relied exclusively on patient self-reports. While this is the primary method for capturing subjective perceptions, it is susceptible to recall bias and social desirability bias. Although we took steps to minimize this by assuring confidentiality and emphasizing the value of honest responses, we acknowledge that participants may have still over-reported their adherence to “please” the medical establishment. Triangulating interview data with objective measures such as weight records or food diaries in future studies could provide a more comprehensive and profound understanding. Finally, this study focused specifically on the patient perspective and did not incorporate the viewpoints of their family caregivers or healthcare providers. Multistakeholder qualitative research can be conducted in the future, which will help to reveal the complex interpersonal dynamics that affect nutritional behavior.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: First, assessment and communication strategies must be tailored. Clinicians should proactively assess patients’ dominant risk perception style—whether they respond better to objective data (eg., weight trends, lab values) or to tangible bodily changes—and tailor their communication accordingly. To bridge the gap in perceived severity, discussions must move beyond abstract warnings to explicitly link inadequate intake to concrete, feared outcomes such as treatment delays or specific surgical complications (eg., anastomotic leakage). Second, interventions should be designed to systematically amplify benefits and reduce barriers. The perceived benefits of “improved physical condition” can be leveraged by using a “nutrition-symptom diary” to help patients visualize the positive link between intake and daily function. Simultaneously, a multidisciplinary approach is needed to mitigate these barriers. For example, dietitians can provide personalized recipes to manage taste changes, social workers can address financial toxicity and access nutritional supplements, and psychologists can help alleviate the emotional burden and guilt associated with eating difficulties. Third, fostering self-efficacy is significant. Medical staff should coset small, achievable dietary goals with patients (eg., “try to take two more sips today than yesterday”) to create mastery experiences. Acknowledging and celebrating these “small wins” is crucial for building confidence and breaking the cycle of helplessness. Finally, family caregivers should be formally trained and incorporated into nutrition care plans, and where feasible, structured peer support programs should be established. In the future, it is necessary to explore visualized, personalized, simple and easy-to-execute, and sustainable tracking patient dietary education and management systems to promote patients’ dietary compliance behaviors.

metadata.title: Influencing Factors of Dietary Guidance Compliance in Patients with Esophageal Cancer During Preoperative Neoadjuvant Therapy: A Qualitative Study.

text: Six categories and fifteen subcategories were identified. The research findings indicated that dietary compliance was by no means a simple matter of will but rather a dynamic decision-making process. The findings have direct and significant implications for multidisciplinary clinical practice. By systematically leveraging the HBM construct, healthcare teams can co-create a more supportive and effective nutritional care environment. This collaborative, theory-driven approach is essential for transforming dietary management from a passive recommendation into an active, sustainable patient behavior, ultimately optimizing treatment tolerance, surgical outcomes, and overall patient well-being.

text: Keloids are a challenging dermatologic condition characterized by excessive scar formation. Traditional treatments, including intralesional corticosteroids, surgical excision, and conventional cryotherapy, frequently result in an incomplete response.[ 1 ] Cryotherapy, delivered through cryo-spray or single-needle application, has shown limited efficacy due to inconsistent freezing and inadequate depth of penetration. These limitations necessitate a more effective and precise therapeutic approach.[ 2 ]

metadata.title: Cryotherapy for Keloids Redefined: The Role of Injector Plate in Large Keloid Treatment.

text: The injector plate-based cryotherapy technique involves simultaneous and controlled cryogen delivery through multiple needles inserted into the keloid. The injector plate employed in this procedure was originally designed for mesotherapy applications. The injector plate has been repurposed in an innovative manner to facilitate the delivery of intralesional cryotherapy for larger keloids. This adaptation improved treatment efficacy by enabling uniform cryogen distribution across the lesion, while simultaneously minimizing procedural discomfort.

metadata.title: Cryotherapy for Keloids Redefined: The Role of Injector Plate in Large Keloid Treatment.

text: After obtaining informed consent, the keloid on the anterior chest was aseptically prepared, and local anesthesia was administered. Three 18-gauge needles 1 cm apart attached to the injector plate were inserted into the keloid. The injector plate was subsequently connected to a medical-grade silicone tubing, which is a part of the keloid cryoprobe. This was followed by liquid nitrogen delivery through the injector plate [ Figure 1 ] until freezing of the entire lesion and an additional 5 mm of healthy appearing skin was achieved [ Video 1 ]. Care was taken to protect the skin at the needle exit points by orienting the bevel ends upward. Two freeze–thaw cycles were performed. Significant improvement was observed after three such cycles at 4 weekly intervals, with reduction in keloid size, pruritus, and discomfort [ Figure 2a and 2b ].

metadata.title: Cryotherapy for Keloids Redefined: The Role of Injector Plate in Large Keloid Treatment.

text: Injector plate-based cryotherapy is an innovative, cost-effective, and efficient modality for the treatment of large keloids, addressing the limitations of traditional cryotherapy.
