text: Vein of Galen malformations (VOGMs) are the most common form of cerebral arteriovenous shunt in neonates and infants. They are characterized by a persistent embryonic median prosencephalic vein of Markowski (MPVM), which serves as a venous collector, resulting in a substantial arteriovenous shunt. 1 , 2 There are three subtypes of VOGM: choroidal, mural, and mixed. Choroidal VOGMs are characterized by multiple high-flow arteriovenous fistulas originating from the anterior choroidal, posterior choroidal, anterior cerebral, quadrigeminal, and thalamoperforating arteries, into the anterior aspect of the MPVM. Mural VOGMs involve fewer, low-flow arteriovenous fistulas, typically from the quadrigeminal arteries and posterior choroidal arteries, draining into the inferolateral margin of the MPVM. Choroidal VOGMs tend to be more severe, presenting with refractory cardiac failure and multiorgan dysfunction. In contrast, mural VOGMs generally have a more indolent course and may present later in life with hydrocephalus, venous hypertension, sinus occlusion, or hemorrhage. Mixed-type VOGMs exhibit features of both choroidal and mural subtypes. 3 , 4

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: Initial diagnosis of VOGM may be made prenatally via ultrasonography and confirmed with fetal MRI, which additionally aids in the identification of secondary complications such as hemorrhagic injury in cerebral white matter. 5 , 6 Despite timely diagnosis, across the largest reported series roughly one-half to two-thirds of neonates with VOGMs experienced heart failure refractory to medical therapy necessitating urgent embolization. 7 , 8 Despite endovascular embolization and dedicated care at specialized centers, mortality for patients remains high at an early age. 6

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: Few cases of VOGMs in adult patients have been reported in the literature. However, among those reported, endovascular and microsurgical interventions have shown favorable outcomes. 9 In this report, we present a case with accompanying multimodal imaging of a patient with a history of progressive neurological decline and new-onset seizures who was diagnosed with a VOGM at 47 years of age.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: A 47-year-old Puerto Rican mother of 2 arrived at the emergency department with new-onset seizures and a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 6 (eyes 1, verbal 1, motor 4). Physical examination was remarkable for exophthalmos and markedly pronounced facial veins.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: Further investigation revealed a history of chronically compensated heart failure as well as apparent early-onset dementia of unclear etiology. The latter compromised her professional role as a pharmaceutical assistant to the point where she was forced to retire, despite having completed all her academic pursuits in the past. Soon after retiring, her condition rapidly deteriorated, and workup at an outside institution led to the diagnosis of a cerebral arteriovenous malformation that was initially deemed untreatable.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: On presentation to our institution, initial noncontrast CT imaging ( Fig. 1 ) revealed an extensive intracerebral hemorrhage, an intraventricular hemorrhage, widespread parenchymal calcifications, and hydrocephalus. After emergency placement of an external ventricular drain, multimodal angiographic images were obtained for further evaluation.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: CTA ( Fig. 2A ) showed an extensive arteriovenous shunt with arterial supply from the pericallosal and callosomarginal branches of the anterior cerebral arteries. Additional contributions were noted from the middle and posterior cerebral arteries. The vein of Galen and straight sinus were markedly dilated with calcifications along their walls. Significant filling of intraorbital and facial veins was noted ( Fig. 2B ). Given the size of the malformation and the neurological state of the patient, she was determined to have a poor prognosis; however, the endovascular neurosurgery team was consulted for consideration of further treatment options.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: The patient underwent cerebral angiography ( Figs. 3 and 4 ) to better characterize the shunt, determine interventional alternatives, and assess prognosis. Angiography demonstrated a brisk arteriovenous shunt into the anterior aspect of the MPVM as seen in choroidal VOGMs, with retrograde venous drainage into facial vessels and poor visualization of additional parenchymal vessels. The diagnosis of a choroidal VOGM was made.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: On evaluation the following day, the patient had a GCS score of 5T (eyes 1, verbal 1, motor 3). Electroencephalography showed a background of low-voltage, poorly modulated, generalized 3- to 4-Hz generalized delta activity compatible with an encephalopathic process, but no evidence of seizure activity.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: On hospitalization day 5, the patient showed an improved GCS score of 8T (eyes 2, verbal 1, motor 5). The family was provided education regarding treatment alternatives, including endovascular embolization, as well as the patient’s prognosis and recovery expectations. However, the family expressed their wishes to explore withdrawal of care. Over the following days, the patient’s GCS score fluctuated, eventually reaching 4T, and the patient’s family opted for withdrawal of care.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: The diagnosis of a choroidal VOGM in an adult is extremely rare. We present the case of a 47-year-old female patient who presented with a history of heart failure and progressive cognitive decline at a young age. It is of particular interest to us how, despite being born with this progressive and devastating congenital condition, the patient was able to have two successful pregnancies, complete professional studies, and live independently until the age of 45 years.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: There are multiple observations worth highlighting from this interesting case. First, several potential key interventions could have altered the disease course at a young age. The patient grew up in a system with reliable access to adequate medical care, raising the question of whether the failure to diagnose this patient’s condition stemmed from a failure to recognize systemic associations or from a failure to understand the condition itself. Naturally, the concern of improper prenatal care in this patient’s mother arises, as this pathology could have been identified during prenatal ultrasonography screening. 8 Second, we highlight this patient’s earlier misdiagnosis of a cerebral arteriovenous malformation. Of particular concern is the lack of consideration of potential treatments, even though earlier endovascular or microsurgical intervention may have altered this patient’s disease course. 9 , – , 11

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: VOGMs have been reported to present with different symptoms in adults than in neonates, with previous cases reporting symptoms of headaches, vomiting, dizziness, vertigo, seizures, loss of consciousness, and focal neurological deficits. 9 , 12 , 13 To our knowledge, slowly progressing cognitive decline and early-onset dementia have not been previously described secondary to adult-presenting choroidal VOGM. More common etiologies of dementia, such as Alzheimer’s disease, should remain high on the list of differentials of patients presenting with dementia at an early age. 14 However, this case sheds light on an atypical cause of cognitive decline: VOGMs. It has been previously described that vascular intracranial malformations cause cerebral hypoperfusion and may present with global cerebral dysfunction and dementia. 15 This underscores the importance of a comprehensive neurological workup, including neurovascular imaging, in patients presenting with cognitive decline, particularly those with early-onset dementia symptoms.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: Despite this case being an anomaly, we believe recent advances in endovascular intervention techniques would have allowed for adequate treatment, which may have prevented this patient’s eventually lethal intracerebral hemorrhage. A systematic review of VOGMs by Udine et al. demonstrated that interventions in VOGM in adult cases tend to have a very favorable outcome, with only 2 of their identified patients having died after undergoing treatment. 9 We understand that, regardless of the age of presentation, this is a complex lesion that signifies a risk for the patient’s life, and treatment should be discussed with the patient and considered whenever feasible. Additionally, early intervention should be favored as the timing of hemorrhagic events in these cases is unpredictable, and delays in treatment may lead to catastrophic and irreversible sequelae, as was the case in our patient. 16

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: Our case is limited by its single-case nature and by the family’s decision not to pursue further treatment, which limits our evaluation of additional interventions. Additionally, there was no record of neuropsychological testing prior to the patient’s decline, limiting our ability to provide objective measurements of the patient’s cognitive function. Similarly, prior imaging was unavailable, and we are therefore unable to report on the radiographic evolution of this atypical pathology. This case, however, is a valuable contribution to the limited number of reported adult VOGM cases in the literature, with a unique presentation mimicking neurodegenerative disease rather than classic symptoms. We also present multimodal imaging studies that contribute to the diagnostic clarity and clinical severity of the malformation. Finally, we highlight a critical gap in early recognition of the pathology and decades of missed diagnosis, which critically impacted the patient’s life and outcome.

metadata.title: Adult presentation of a vein of Galen malformation as cognitive decline in a 47-year-old multiparous female: illustrative case.

text: We report the rare case of a 47-year-old Puerto Rican female who, despite having a history of congestive heart failure and a recent diagnosis of early-onset dementia, never received care for the likely underlying cause of her symptoms, a VOGM, which eventually led to her death. Multiple key lessons can be drawn from this unfortunate case. First, although VOGMs have a poor prognosis and are typically detected on antenatal ultrasonography, they may still present in adult patients. Second, while newborns primarily present with congestive heart failure, adult patients with VOGM may have atypical presentations, such as this patient, who experienced progressive cognitive decline likely due to global cerebral hypoperfusion. Additionally, the diagnosis of a VOGM in adults is challenging and requires advanced imaging modalities such as CTA and DSA, and expert opinions from specialists in highly specialized fields such as pediatric neuroradiology should be sought. Finally, when a VOGM is diagnosed in an adult patient, early endovascular or open microsurgical intervention should be strongly considered to avoid potentially lethal sequelae.

text: Core Tip: Depression is indeed common in individuals with osteoarthritis. Depression is not only correlated with sleep disorders, pain, and functional prognosis, but also with cognitive decline. Cognitive decline can be an obstacle to rehabilitation after total knee arthroplasty, leading to a decline in quality of life due to functional decline. Therefore, it’s crucial for healthcare professionals to assess and address both the physical and mental health needs of people with osteoarthritis.

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: Many older individuals suffer from chronic knee joint pain due to osteoarthritis, a degenerative joint disease. Knee joint pain often limits mobility, making it difficult to walk, climb stairs, or perform daily activities. Total knee arthroplasty (TKA), also known as total knee replacement, is a surgical procedure designed to replace a damaged knee joint with artificial implants. TKA can significantly reduce or eliminate this pain. TKA can restore lost mobility, allowing individuals to regain independence. By reducing pain and improving mobility, TKA can significantly enhance an older person’s overall quality of life, enabling them to participate in social activities and enjoy a more active lifestyle.

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: However, older people may require a more tailored and prolonged rehabilitation program after TKA. Motivation and cognitive function are essential for rehabilitation. Depression or cognitive decline can affect the therapeutic outcome of TKA in older people. A recent study that analyzed the functional prognosis of 100 older people who underwent TKA identified substantial disparities in cognitive impairment among individuals diagnosed with depression[ 1 ]. In this paper, we explore the extent to which cognitive function and depression in older individuals undergoing TKA should be given consideration.

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: The global population is aging, leading to a higher prevalence of osteoarthritis, the primary condition requiring TKA. Advances in surgical techniques and implant technology have made TKA safer and more effective, expanding its applicability to older patients. Greater awareness of TKA as a treatment option and improved access to orthopedic care have contributed to its increased utilization. There’s a clear trend of TKA being performed on increasingly older patients[ 2 ]. While age was once a greater limiting factor, modern techniques and better patient management have changed this. In general, TKA has demonstrated very positive outcomes in older people, with significant pain relief and improved function. Even very older individuals, including those in their nineties, can experience substantial benefits from TKA[ 2 ].

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: However, older people have a higher risk of postoperative complications, including: Increased risk of infection, increased risk of cardiovascular complications, increased risk of falls, and increased risk of readmission[ 3 ]. Therefore, preoperative assessment and optimization of comorbidities are crucial to minimize these risks. Factors that can affect prognosis include patient’s overall health and comorbidities, bone quality, adherence to rehabilitation, and the presence of cognitive impairment[ 4 ]. TKA is a viable and often very successful option for older people with severe knee osteoarthritis. While risks are higher in this population, careful patient selection, preoperative optimization, and tailored rehabilitation can lead to significant improvements in pain and function. The impact of physical function and bone condition on surgical indications has been examined, but little research has been done on risk assessment of mental function. In the study by Nicolino et al [ 1 ], the occurrence of depression was associated with decline in cognitive function and frailty, but it had little effect on surgical outcomes and did not narrow the indications for TKA, which is good news for older people.

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: Cognitive assessment plays a crucial role in the preoperative evaluation of older people undergoing TKA. Cognitive impairment in patients undergoing TKA is associated with an increased risk of postoperative delirium (POD)[ 5 ], worsened postoperative functional status, and discharge to non-home facility[ 6 ]. POD was most common in older patients with baseline cognitive impairment. Individuals with POD had higher rates of 90-day postoperative surgical and joint-related complications. POD can lead to increased hospital stay, complications, and poorer functional outcomes.

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: Cognitive function influences a patient’s ability to understand and follow postoperative rehabilitation protocols. Impaired cognition can hinder their participation in physical therapy, affecting their recovery. Cognitive assessment helps determine a patient’s capacity to understand the risks and benefits of TKA and provide informed consent. This ensures that patients are making autonomous decisions about their care. Identifying cognitive impairment allows healthcare providers to tailor postoperative care to the patient’s needs. This may involve simplified instructions, increased support, and closer monitoring. Cognitive status can influence functional outcomes and patient satisfaction after TKA. Preoperative assessment can help identify patients at risk for poorer outcomes and guide interventions. Older people are at higher risk of having some form of cognitive decline. The assessment of cognitive function in older people scheduled for preoperative TKA requires a multifaceted approach combining standardized tests such as the mini-mental state examination, Montreal Cognitive Assessment, and clock drawing with the patient’s medical history[ 7 ]. Cognitive assessment in individuals undergoing TKA has been demonstrated to optimize patient safety, improve postoperative outcomes, and ensure that patients receive appropriate care throughout the surgical process.

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: The relationship between depression and TKA outcomes in older adults is complex and has garnered increasing attention in recent years. Associations between preoperative depression and TKA outcomes include increased pain perception, impaired functional recovery, and increased risk of complications. Depression can heighten pain sensitivity, leading to patients reporting more intense postoperative pain[ 8 ]. This can negatively impact their perception of recovery and satisfaction with the procedure. Depression can reduce motivation and adherence to rehabilitation programs, resulting in poorer functional outcomes[ 9 ]. Patients may have difficulty engaging in physical therapy and regaining mobility. Some studies suggest that preoperative depression may increase the risk of postoperative complications, such as infections and delayed wound healing[ 10 ]. This can be due to the negative mindset that is related to depression. Depression inherently reduces quality of life, and when combined with the stress of surgery and recovery, can severely impact the patients’ overall well-being. Moreover, postoperative depression can further hinder rehabilitation efforts and slow down recovery[ 11 ]. It can exacerbate pain and disability, leading to a cycle of negative reinforcement. Postoperative depression can decrease a patients will to participate in their rehabilitation[ 11 ].

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: Routine screening for depression in older adults undergoing TKA is essential. Tools like the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 and the Geriatric Depression Scale can be used[ 12 ]. A multidisciplinary approach, in which orthopedic surgeons, geriatricians, and mental health professionals share these indicators, is imperative. This approach allows for comprehensive assessment and management of both physical and mental health. Addressing depression through preoperative and postoperative interventions, such as therapy and medication, may improve TKA outcomes. Optimizing mental health can enhance patient motivation, adherence to rehabilitation, and overall satisfaction[ 13 ]. Social isolation, and lack of social support can greatly increase the risk of depression, which then negatively effects TKA outcomes. Depression can significantly impact TKA outcomes in older adults, affecting pain perception, functional recovery, and overall satisfaction[ 14 ]. Therefore, it is crucial to address depression as part of the comprehensive care of these patients.

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: Depression is indeed common in individuals with osteoarthritis. Research has consistently shown a significant association between these two conditions[ 15 ]. Osteoarthritis causes chronic pain, which can significantly impact a person’s daily life, limiting their mobility and independence. This chronic pain and disability can lead to feelings of frustration, helplessness, and hopelessness, contributing to depression. The limitations imposed by osteoarthritis can affect a person’s ability to participate in social activities, hobbies, and work. This reduction in quality of life can negatively impact mental well-being and increase the risk of depression. Some research suggests that the inflammatory processes involved in osteoarthritis may also play a role in the development of depression[ 16 ]. There are research papers that show that inflammatory cytokines are involved in both osteoarthritis and depression[ 17 ]. Pain from osteoarthritis can disrupt sleep patterns, leading to insomnia and poor sleep quality[ 18 ]. Sleep disturbances are a known risk factor for depression. The psychological impact of living with a chronic condition like osteoarthritis can also contribute to depression. This includes things like dealing with the change of body image, and the feeling of loss of control. Therefore, it’s crucial for healthcare professionals to assess and address both the physical and mental health needs of patients with osteoarthritis.

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: There is a consensus that both physical and mental considerations are necessary for patients undergoing TKA. However, further research is necessary to determine the extent to which each factor has an impact. In the study by Nicolino et al [ 1 ], depression did not have a significant impact on the outcome of surgery. However, the study was conducted retrospectively, and the subjects were patients who underwent TKA, which introduces the possibility of selection bias. To address this gap in knowledge, it is recommended that future studies augment the number of cases and employ multivariate analysis, incorporating variables such as age, sex, preoperative nutritional status, bone condition, activity level, pain severity, cognitive function, and depression severity. This approach would facilitate a comprehensive examination of whether mental function serves as an explanatory factor for the outcomes of surgical procedures. Statistical techniques, such as regression analysis, are employed to ascertain the independent effect of psychological factors on TKA outcomes, while controlling for other potential confounding variables ( e.g. , age, severity of osteoarthritis, comorbidities)[ 19 ]. Correlation analysis can be used to examine the relationships between specific psychological factors and TKA outcomes[ 20 ]. Alternatively, a prospective study is necessary to examine how depression affects the outcome of surgery, with cases divided according to the severity of depression under strict physical surgical indications. In addition, intervention trials are required to ascertain whether interventions for depression have a positive effect on the outcome of surgery. Researchers can conduct interventional studies to evaluate the effectiveness of psychological interventions ( e.g. , cognitive-behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based interventions) in improving TKA outcomes[ 21 ].

metadata.title: Considering mental function for successful total knee arthroplasty in older people.

text: The relationship between physical pain and mental well-being is a complex and multifaceted one. Pain has been demonstrated to exacerbate depression, and depression has been shown to worsen pain perception. A failure to address either aspect may impede the successful management of the other. Addressing both physical and mental health can lead to enhanced pain management, improved function, and an elevated quality of life for older individuals afflicted with osteoarthritis. A holistic approach, which acknowledges the patient as a whole person rather than merely a set of symptoms, is essential for effective treatment. This holistic approach fosters a sense of empowerment, leading to enhanced overall well-being. Conducting comprehensive assessments that encompass pain levels, functional limitations, and psychological well-being ( e.g. , screening for depression and anxiety) is paramount. Involving a multidisciplinary team, comprising physicians, physical therapists, occupational therapists, psychologists, and social workers, is also paramount. The implementation of effective pain management strategies, including surgical interventions such as TKA, the administration of medication, physical therapy, and other non-pharmacological approaches, is paramount. Furthermore, it is essential to facilitate access to mental health services, such as counseling or therapy, to address depression, anxiety, and other psychological concerns. Furthermore, patients should be educated about osteoarthritis, pain management techniques, and coping strategies for dealing with the emotional challenges of the condition. The establishment of open communication and the demonstration of empathy are crucial in fostering trust and rapport with patients. Patients’ concerns, both physical and mental, must be actively listened to. Regular follow-up appointments are crucial for monitoring the patient’s physical and mental health progress and adjusting treatment plans as necessary.
